text:
What if... mung bean seeds are kept in a concentrated solution after soaking in water for 12 hours? What will happen to them?

What do you infer? What do you expect in terms of changes in their weight? The weight of the potato piece in Beaker A has increased, while the weight of the potato piece in Beaker B has decreased. This happens because the cell membrane allows water to move in and out of the cell but not the sugar or salt molecules. Water moves from an area with more solute and less solute (dilute solution) to an area with less water and more solute (concentrated solution) until the concentrations in the two areas become equal. This movement of water through a selectively permeable membrane is called osmosis. In plants, water from the soil enters root cells by this process of osmosis.

What if... a cell is kept in salt or sugar solutions of different concentrations?

What do you know? The cell membrane is extremely thin, about 7 to 10 nanometres (nm) thick (1 nanometre = 0.000001 mm). It is made up of lipids (fats) and proteins. The fluid-mosaic model explains its structure.

• The molecules can move sideways, flip and rotate within the membrane. Hence, it is fluid.
• Proteins in the membrane act like gatekeepers in helping substances pass through.

diagrams.label: Structure of a cell membrane
diagrams.description: A diagram showing a cross-section of a cell membrane. The structure is composed of a lipid bilayer, which forms the main boundary. Various types of proteins are embedded within this bilayer, spanning across it.

diagrams.label: Effect of solutions of different concentrations on a cell
diagrams.description: This diagram illustrates four scenarios involving a plant cell and external solutions. The cell is shown in its original state (Isotonic solution). In the second scenario (Hypotonic solution), the cell swells and appears turgid, indicating water moving into the cell. In the third scenario (Hypertonic solution), the cell shrinks and pulls away from the surrounding structure, indicating water moving out of the cell (plasmolysis). The first and fourth scenarios show the cell in an Isotonic solution, maintaining its normal shape.
